title: The Land of Too Much: American Abundance and the Paradox of Poverty By Monica Prasad Harvard University Press. 2012. 344 pages. $39.95 cloth
abstract: The Land of Too Much builds a complex argument around a deceptively simple insight: that scholars have not properly considered how the late-nineteenth-century American agricultural boom shaped the structure of political institutions in both the United States and Europe. According to Monica Prasad, when American produce swamped world markets, European governments responded by turning to protectionism and welfare spending, a move designed to promote exports while also appeasing farmers and workers. American farmers' staggering productivity had very different political consequences back in the United States. The same agricultural boom empowered farmers, allowing them to imprint their interests and worries upon early 20th-century state institutions. Because farmers distrusted eastern finance and big business, the United States had stricter regulation and less branch banking. Because farmers were progressive, regressive sales taxes did not get a strong foothold in the United States. Following the ramifications of these policies forward in history, Prasad argues that these early policies had consequences that their proponents could never have foreseen. Without the regressive sales taxes pulling in funds, the American welfare state floundered. Without a welfare state to provide additional support, Americans came to rely on credit to meet basic needs for shelter, education, and healthcare. Without branch banking to add stability, American finance became more vulnerable to crises. In the 1970s, when big business felt the squeeze of economic pressure, its advocates made the case that the United States must adopt the looser regulations of European nations to keep up. What no one seemed to notice was that laxer European business and banking rules were balanced by tax and welfare policies that cooled the economy through other means. Without that balance, the removal of banking regulations allowed US financial markets to expand in dangerous ways.
